docs.title: 目的
docs.text: 本标准描述 Docker 主机的安装、维护、清理和恢复实践。

docs.title: 适用场景
docs.text: 本标准适用于单机 Docker 工作负载、开发主机、轻量内部服务和构建机器。生产编排场景应优先使用 Kubernetes 或其他托管调度器。

docs.title: 安装原则
docs.text:
1. 从可信仓库安装 Docker。
  2. 当可复现性重要时固定版本。
  3. 将 Docker data root 放在容量充足的卷上。
  4. 在运行长期容器前配置日志大小限制。
  5. 避免直接从未经评审的 Compose 文件运行关键业务服务。

docs.title: 主机准备
docs.text:
sudo mkdir -p /etc/docker
sudo mkdir -p /var/lib/docker

推荐 daemon 配置：

{
  "log-driver": "json-file",
  "log-opts": {
    "max-size": "100m",
    "max-file": "3"
  },
  "storage-driver": "overlay2"
}

应用配置：

sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable --now docker
docker info

docs.title: 日常维护
docs.text:
检查磁盘使用：

docker system df
df -h /var/lib/docker

列出资源：

docker ps -a
docker images
docker volume ls
docker network ls

docs.title: 磁盘压力清理（受控）
docs.text:
当构建缓存、旧镜像、已停止容器或未使用网络占用磁盘时使用。

前置条件：

  • 已查看 docker system df，确认哪些空间确实可回收。
  • 已识别关键镜像和容器。
  • 当前发布的回滚镜像已确认在本地或镜像仓库可用。
  • 卷清理已明确批准，或明确排除。

安全清理顺序（风险从低到高）：

docker container prune -f   # 1. 已停止容器
docker network prune -f     # 2. 未使用网络
docker image prune -f       # 3. dangling 镜像（不会删除有 tag 的镜像）
docker builder prune -f     # 4. 构建缓存

回收“旧”的带 tag 镜像需要使用 docker image prune -a。它会删除所有没有被运行中容器使用的镜像，包括前置条件中识别出的回滚镜像。只有在确认这些回滚镜像仍可从镜像仓库获得，并且得到明确批准后才能使用：

# 高风险：删除所有未使用镜像，包括本地回滚镜像。
# 只有在确认回滚镜像可从镜像仓库获得后使用。
docker image prune -a

除非已明确知道卷的归属和数据生命周期，否则跳过卷清理。docker volume prune 特意不带 -f，让交互确认保留为删除有状态数据前的最后一道保护：

# 高风险：仅在明确批准且确认卷可丢弃时使用。
docker volume prune

验证：

  • docker system df 显示回收了预期空间。
  • 运行中的容器仍然健康。
  • 回滚镜像仍在本地或镜像仓库可用。
  • 磁盘告警恢复。

失败模式：

失败模式 原因 失去快速回滚能力 删除了回滚所需镜像 数据丢失 清理了保存状态数据的卷 发布被中断 在活跃发布期间执行清理

docs.title: Docker 网络恢复与重置
docs.text: Docker 网络问题的症状包括容器无法解析名称、bridge 网络冲突、IP 段变更后服务不可达，或 bridge CIDR 配置错误。

docs.title: 低风险检查（先执行）
docs.text:
docker network ls
docker network inspect bridge
ip addr
iptables -S

docs.title: 低风险恢复
docs.text:
1. 停止受影响的非关键容器。
  2. 只删除未使用的 Docker 网络。
  3. 重启 Docker。
  4. 基于已评审的 Compose 或部署文件重建容器。

docs.title: 受控 bridge 重置（较高风险）
docs.text:
只有当 bridge 损坏、与主机路由冲突，或使用了错误 CIDR 时，才执行完整 bridge 重置。

前置条件：

  • 维护窗口已批准。
  • 已识别运行中的容器。
  • 已备份 /etc/docker/daemon.json。
  • 新 bridge CIDR 已选择，并确认不与主机、VPN、Kubernetes 和云网络地址段冲突。
  • 远程访问不依赖即将重置的 Docker bridge。

步骤：

  1. 干净停止 Docker（systemctl stop docker）。
  2. 确认没有关键容器被期望继续运行。
  3. 使用批准的 bridge CIDR（"bip" 或 "default-address-pools"）和日志轮转设置更新 /etc/docker/daemon.json。
  4. 启动 Docker（systemctl start docker）。正常情况下，Docker 会在启动时根据新配置重建默认 bridge，不需要手工删除 bridge。
  5. 只有当 Docker 停止后旧的 docker0 接口仍然存在，并阻塞新 CIDR 时，才在 Docker 已停止 的状态下显式删除它，绝不要在 Docker 运行时删除： 
    sudo ip link set docker0 down
sudo ip link delete docker0
  6. 验证 bridge 地址、容器网络、NAT 和服务可达性。

回滚：从备份恢复上一版 daemon.json，然后重启 Docker。

不要把删除 /var/lib/docker 当成常规修复步骤。数据删除是单独动作，需要自己的备份和批准关口；绝不能混入常规网络修复。

docs.title: Compose 文件安全
docs.text:
运行 Compose stack 前：

  • 评审镜像和 tag。
  • 将硬编码凭据替换为环境变量或 Secret 引用。
  • 确认卷和 bind mount。
  • 确认暴露端口。
  • 增加日志限制。
  • 增加重启策略。
  • 为有状态服务记录备份与恢复方式。

docs.title: 验证
docs.text:
docker version
docker info
docker run --rm hello-world
docker system df

docs.title: 相关文档
docs.text:
• Linux 服务器基线标准
  • 发布安全检查清单

docs.title: 目的
docs.text: 本标准描述 Harbor 或类似私有容器镜像仓库的安全运维方式。

docs.title: 核心控制
docs.text:
• 启用身份认证。
  • 使用项目级权限。
  • 在可用时启用漏洞扫描。
  • 配置留存策略。
  • 备份 registry 元数据和镜像存储。
  • 所有 registry 流量使用 TLS。
  • 不公开 robot account token。

docs.title: 部署检查清单
docs.text: 区域 检查项 TLS 证书有效，且续期流程已记录。 Storage 对象存储或持久卷已纳入备份。 Access 管理员账号受保护，robot account 权限收敛。 Retention 未使用 tag 被安全清理。 Replication 如使用复制，凭据被安全保存。

docs.title: 验证
docs.text:
docker login registry.example.com
docker pull registry.example.com/example/app:1.0.0
docker push registry.example.com/example/app:1.0.1

docs.title: 相关文档
docs.text:
• Harbor 证书轮换 Runbook
  • TLS 证书过期 Runbook

docs.title: 目的
docs.text: 本标准总结 APISIX、Kong 或基于 Nginx 的 Ingress Gateway 等 Kubernetes API Gateway 的安全运维实践。

docs.title: Gateway 职责
docs.text:
API Gateway 通常负责：

  • 路由。
  • TLS 终止。
  • 认证与授权集成。
  • 限流。
  • 请求与响应转换。
  • 可观测性。
  • 金丝雀或权重路由。

docs.title: 部署检查清单
docs.text:
生产使用前确认：

  • Gateway controller 高可用。
  • Data plane Pod 配置了 readiness 和 liveness probe。
  • Admin API 没有公开暴露。
  • Dashboard 访问受保护。
  • TLS 证书生命周期已记录。
  • 指标由 Prometheus 抓取。
  • 访问日志已路由到日志平台。
  • 限流和超时默认值已定义。

docs.title: 路由模板
docs.text:
公开示例使用占位符：

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: example-gateway-route
  namespace: example-namespace
spec:
  ingressClassName: example-gateway
  rules:
    - host: api.example.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /v1
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: example-api
                port:
                  number: 80

docs.title: 安全控制
docs.text:
• 禁用匿名 admin 访问。
  • 通过 NetworkPolicy 限制 admin 端点。
  • 将 admin key 存储在 Secret Manager 中。
  • 内部 API 和外部 API 使用独立路由。
  • 强制设置请求大小和超时限制。
  • 为路由变更启用审计日志。

docs.title: 可观测性
docs.text:
最低指标：

  • 请求速率。
  • 错误率。
  • 延迟分位数。
  • Upstream 健康状态。
  • Gateway Pod CPU 和内存。
  • 路由级 4xx 与 5xx 数量。

docs.title: Kubernetes 部署画像
docs.text:
将 Kubernetes API Gateway（例如 APISIX）视为平台组件，而不是单个应用的依赖。它的故障或错误配置会影响其背后的所有路由。

生产流量接入前，先确定：

  • 暴露模型：NodePort、LoadBalancer，或由 ingress 管理。
  • 控制面 datastore 拓扑和持久化方式，例如 etcd 成员及其存储。
  • 所有持久状态使用的 StorageClass 与回收策略。
  • Admin API 访问边界。
  • Dashboard 认证与网络限制。
  • Prometheus 指标暴露方式。
  • Gateway 及其 datastore 的资源限制。

生产判断：

  • Admin API 保持内部访问并受网络限制，绝不公开暴露。
  • 避免使用通配 admin allowlist。
  • 不要把 admin token 或 key 提交到 values 文件；使用 Secret Manager。
  • 固定 chart 和 gateway 版本。
  • 在承载生产流量前验证 datastore（例如 etcd）健康。
  • 将插件和路由配置视为受变更管理的平台配置。

docs.title: 部署验证
docs.text:
• Datastore 成员（例如 etcd）健康。
  • Gateway Pod 为 Ready，且 gateway Service 能接收流量。
  • Admin API 只能从允许的网络路径访问。
  • Prometheus 端点暴露 gateway 指标。
  • 能创建、访问并干净删除一条测试路由。

docs.title: 变更流程
docs.text:
1. 评审路由 manifest。
  2. 验证 Service 和端口。
  3. 应用到预发环境。
  4. 执行冒烟测试。
  5. 应用到生产环境。
  6. 确认指标和日志。
  7. 记录回滚命令。

回滚：

kubectl -n example-namespace rollout undo deployment/example-api
kubectl -n example-namespace delete ingress example-gateway-route

docs.title: 相关文档
docs.text:
• Kubernetes 存储与 Ingress 标准
  • Prometheus 告警标准

docs.title: 系统设计
docs.text:
系统设计回答一个问题：一套系统能不能在真实流量、真实故障和真实组织约束下运行。

架构文章必须解释约束、备选方案、失败模式、验证和回滚。一张组件图远远不够。

docs.title: 生产架构
docs.text:
• AWS EKS 生产级高可用集群设计与交付标准 —— 全站旗舰，生产深度的质量基准。
  • Kubernetes 集群供给标准
  • Kubernetes 存储与 Ingress 标准
  • kubeadm 安装标准
  • EKS 与 Kubernetes 升级标准
  • NFS PVC 容量管理

docs.title: 阅读建议
docs.text: 如果你想用一篇文章判断这个站点的深度，直接读 EKS 高可用集群标准。它从结论先行、适用与不适用边界、假设声明、失败模式到回滚路径，是后续所有内容的质量模板。

docs.title: AWS EKS 生产级高可用集群设计与交付标准
结论先行
docs.text:
在 AWS 上搭建一个生产级 EKS 高可用集群，重点不是把节点数量调大，也不是把 Kubernetes 控制面创建出来。真正的难点是把控制面、节点、网络、身份权限、入口流量、存储、发布、观测、升级和故障恢复放进同一个可验证的交付模型里。

一个可信的 EKS 高可用方案至少要做到：

  • 集群跨多个 Availability Zone 运行。
  • 工作负载可以在单个 AZ 故障时继续调度。
  • 节点容量可以自动恢复和扩缩。
  • Pod 权限不依赖节点级长期凭证。
  • Ingress、DNS、证书、日志、指标、告警都有明确责任边界。
  • 每个关键变更都有验证方法和回滚路径。

如果一个方案只写 eksctl create cluster，它只能证明集群能创建，不能证明它能承载生产。

docs.title: 适用场景
docs.text:
本文适用于以下场景：

  • 新建面向生产环境的 Amazon EKS 集群。
  • 将已有虚拟机或自建 Kubernetes 迁移到 EKS。
  • 为现有 EKS 集群补齐高可用、权限、安全、监控和发布标准。
  • 为团队制定可复用的 EKS 交付检查清单。

本文不适用于：

  • 单人实验环境。
  • 短期 PoC。
  • 对 Kubernetes 运行机制完全不了解的入门教程。
  • 要求跨 Region 零停机容灾的金融级多活系统。跨 Region 是另一个设计问题，不能用普通多 AZ 集群替代。

docs.title: 基本假设
docs.text:
本文采用以下假设：

  • 集群运行在一个 AWS Region 内，至少使用 3 个 AZ。
  • 工作负载以无状态服务为主，有少量状态服务通过托管数据库、对象存储或持久卷承载。
  • 团队使用 GitOps 或 CI/CD 进行变更交付。
  • 集群需要公开或半公开入口流量。
  • 集群需要满足最小权限、可审计和可回滚要求。

如果你的业务只有几个低风险服务，可以先降低复杂度：两类节点组、一个入口控制器、基础指标和日志即可。不要为了“看起来生产级”一开始就堆满所有组件。

docs.title: 目标架构
docs.text:
Users
  |
Route 53
  |
AWS Load Balancer Controller
  |
Ingress / Service
  |
Application Pods
  |
Managed data services / persistent storage

Control and operations plane:

EKS managed control plane
  - multi-AZ managed by AWS

Worker capacity
  - managed node groups or Karpenter
  - spread across at least 3 AZs
  - separate system and application capacity

Identity
  - IAM roles for cluster operators
  - EKS Pod Identity or IRSA for workload permissions

Observability
  - metrics
  - logs
  - events
  - alerts
  - audit trail

核心原则：AWS 托管 EKS 控制面，但业务高可用仍然取决于你的 VPC、子网、节点组、调度策略、Pod 副本、PDB、存储、入口和发布方式。

docs.title: 1. 多 AZ 是底线，不是亮点
docs.text:
生产集群至少使用 3 个 AZ。只使用 2 个 AZ 时，很多故障仍然能扛住，但容量恢复、调度弹性和维护窗口会更紧。只使用 1 个 AZ 的 EKS 不能称为高可用生产集群。

需要检查：

  • VPC 是否覆盖至少 3 个 AZ。
  • 每个 AZ 是否有私有子网。
  • 节点组是否跨 AZ。
  • 应用副本是否通过 topology spread 或反亲和规则分散。
  • 存储类是否理解 AZ 绑定特性。

常见失败模式：集群使用了 3 个 AZ，但节点组只在 1 个 AZ 中有容量。页面上看起来是多 AZ，实际调度时仍然单点。

docs.title: 2. 节点容量要分层
docs.text:
不要把所有 Pod 都跑在同一类节点上。最低限度应分成：

  • system pool：CoreDNS、Ingress Controller、日志采集、监控组件。
  • application pool：业务服务。
  • batch or spot pool：可中断任务、异步任务、低优先级任务。

分层的目的不是复杂化，而是防止业务突增把系统组件挤掉。

推荐原则：

  • 系统组件使用稳定容量。
  • 业务服务根据资源画像选择实例族。
  • 可中断任务可以使用 Spot，但必须能重试。
  • 关键服务配置 PDB，避免维护时被一次性驱逐。

docs.title: 3. Pod 权限不要继承节点权限
docs.text:
生产集群不应让 Pod 依赖节点 IAM Role 获得 AWS 权限。应使用 EKS Pod Identity 或 IRSA，为每类工作负载绑定最小权限 IAM Role。

判断标准：

  • 一个 Pod 只拿到它需要的 AWS API 权限。
  • Secret 管理、对象存储、消息队列、镜像仓库访问分开授权。
  • 权限变更可审计。
  • 删除应用时可以同步删除对应权限。

常见失败模式：为了省事，把 S3、ECR、CloudWatch、Secrets Manager 权限都挂到节点角色上。短期能跑，长期会让任意被攻破的 Pod 拥有过大横向移动能力。

docs.title: 4. Ingress 不是流量入口的全部
docs.text:
一个生产入口链路至少要明确：

  • DNS 由谁管理。
  • TLS 证书在哪里签发和续期。
  • 负载均衡器是公网还是内网。
  • WAF 是否需要。
  • 健康检查路径是否稳定。
  • 灰度或回滚如何切流。

如果只创建 Ingress YAML，没有回答这些问题，入口层还没有设计完成。

docs.title: 5. 状态数据优先用托管服务
docs.text:
EKS 可以运行状态服务，但生产系统不要默认把数据库、消息队列、搜索引擎都塞进集群。优先考虑 RDS、ElastiCache、MSK、OpenSearch、S3 等托管服务。

在集群内运行状态服务之前，需要回答：

  • 数据备份在哪里。
  • 跨 AZ 恢复如何做。
  • 升级失败如何回滚。
  • 节点丢失时数据如何恢复。
  • 团队是否真的有运维该组件的能力。

docs.title: 阶段 1：网络和账号边界
docs.text:
交付内容：

  • VPC、子网、路由表、NAT Gateway 或替代方案。
  • 集群 IAM Role。
  • 节点 IAM Role。
  • 管理员和 CI/CD 访问边界。
  • 标签规范。

验证：

aws sts get-caller-identity
aws ec2 describe-subnets --filters "Name=vpc-id,Values=<vpc-id>"
aws eks describe-cluster --name <cluster-name>

风险点：NAT Gateway 成本容易被低估；私有集群访问路径容易被忘记；过宽的管理员权限会在后续阶段变成默认做法。

docs.title: 阶段 2：创建 EKS 控制面
docs.text:
交付内容：

  • EKS 集群。
  • Kubernetes 版本选择。
  • API Server endpoint 访问策略。
  • 日志开关。
  • 加密配置。

验证：

aws eks update-kubeconfig --name <cluster-name> --region <region>
kubectl cluster-info
kubectl get nodes
kubectl get ns

注意：刚创建完控制面时，kubectl get nodes 可能为空。控制面健康不等于节点已经加入。

docs.title: 阶段 3：节点容量和系统组件
docs.text:
交付内容：

  • Managed Node Groups 或 Karpenter。
  • CoreDNS、kube-proxy、VPC CNI 版本确认。
  • 系统组件和业务组件的调度隔离。
  • Cluster Autoscaler 或 Karpenter 扩缩容策略。

验证：

kubectl get nodes -o wide
kubectl -n kube-system get pods -o wide
kubectl describe node <node-name>
kubectl top nodes

失败模式：节点 Ready，但 CNI IP 不足，业务 Pod 卡在 Pending。此时问题不在 Deployment，而在子网地址容量或 CNI 配置。

docs.title: 阶段 4：身份、权限和密钥
docs.text:
交付内容：

  • Pod Identity 或 IRSA。
  • 最小权限 IAM Policy。
  • Secret 管理策略。
  • CI/CD 部署权限。

验证：

kubectl -n <namespace> describe serviceaccount <service-account>
kubectl -n <namespace> exec deploy/<deployment-name> -- aws sts get-caller-identity

判断标准：业务 Pod 看到的身份应该是它自己的工作负载角色，而不是节点角色。

docs.title: 阶段 5：入口、证书和发布路径
docs.text:
交付内容：

  • AWS Load Balancer Controller。
  • IngressClass。
  • DNS 记录。
  • TLS 证书。
  • 健康检查。
  • 灰度或蓝绿发布策略。

验证：

kubectl -n kube-system get deploy aws-load-balancer-controller
kubectl -n <namespace> get ingress
kubectl -n <namespace> describe ingress <ingress-name>
curl -I https://<service-domain>

失败模式：Ingress 创建成功，但 Load Balancer 没有被创建。通常是控制器权限、子网标签或 Ingress 注解错误。

docs.title: 阶段 6：可观测性和告警
docs.text:
交付内容：

  • 节点指标。
  • Pod 指标。
  • 应用日志。
  • Kubernetes Events。
  • API Server 或审计日志。
  • 关键告警。

最低告警集合：

  • 节点 NotReady。
  • Pod CrashLoopBackOff。
  • Deployment 副本不足。
  • PVC 容量高水位。
  • Ingress 5xx 升高。
  • HPA 长时间无法扩容。
  • CoreDNS 错误率升高。

验证：

kubectl get events -A --sort-by=.lastTimestamp
kubectl top pods -A
kubectl -n <monitoring-namespace> get pods

docs.title: 回滚策略
docs.text:
EKS 集群回滚不能只靠 kubectl rollout undo。需要分层处理。

层级 回滚方式 应用发布 回滚镜像、回滚 Helm release、回滚 GitOps commit Ingress 切回旧规则、旧证书或旧负载均衡器 节点 回滚节点组 Launch Template 或创建旧版本节点组 权限 回滚 IAM Policy 或 ServiceAccount 绑定 集群插件 回滚到兼容版本或临时扩容旧节点 Kubernetes 版本 通常不能原地降级，应通过新集群迁移恢复

最容易被忽略的是 Kubernetes 版本升级。升级前必须确认插件、节点 AMI、控制器和关键 CRD 的兼容性。不要把生产集群升级当成普通包升级。

docs.title: 生产验收清单
docs.text:
上线前至少检查：

  • 集群跨 3 个 AZ。
  • 私有子网容量足够。
  • 节点组跨 AZ 且有自动恢复能力。
  • 系统组件和业务组件有容量隔离。
  • CoreDNS 有足够副本并分散调度。
  • 关键应用有多副本、PDB、资源请求和限制。
  • 入口链路有 DNS、TLS、健康检查和回滚方案。
  • Pod 使用最小权限身份。
  • 密钥没有写入镜像、ConfigMap 或 Git。
  • 日志、指标、事件和告警可用。
  • CI/CD 权限可审计。
  • 备份和恢复流程被演练过。
  • 升级和故障演练有记录。

docs.title: 错误 1：把 EKS 托管控制面等同于业务高可用
docs.text: AWS 托管控制面降低了控制面运维成本，但业务 Pod、节点容量、Ingress、存储、发布和监控仍然由你负责。业务高可用不是购买出来的，是设计和验证出来的。

docs.title: 错误 2：只看节点数量，不看调度分布
docs.text: 6 个节点如果都在一个 AZ，遇到 AZ 故障仍然会整体受影响。副本数和节点数必须结合拓扑分布来看。

docs.title: 错误 3：先迁移状态服务
docs.text: 迁移时应先选择无状态服务验证镜像、发布、Ingress、日志、指标和回滚链路。状态服务最后处理，因为它的问题不是能不能调度，而是数据一致性、恢复时间和恢复点。

docs.title: 错误 4：把权限问题留到最后
docs.text: 权限如果最后处理，通常会变成临时授权和长期例外。生产集群应从第一天就设计 Pod 身份和部署权限边界。

docs.title: 问答助手知识块
docs.text:
问：EKS 高可用是不是只要使用 Managed Node Group 就够了？

不是。Managed Node Group 解决节点生命周期的一部分问题，但高可用还依赖多 AZ 子网、调度分散、Pod 副本、PDB、Ingress、存储、权限、监控和回滚。

问：生产 EKS 集群建议几个 AZ？

通常建议至少 3 个 AZ。2 个 AZ 可以用于较低风险场景，但容量恢复和维护弹性较弱。1 个 AZ 不应称为生产高可用。

问：Pod 访问 AWS 服务应该怎么授权？

使用 EKS Pod Identity 或 IRSA，为工作负载绑定最小权限 IAM Role。不要让业务 Pod 继承节点角色上的宽权限。

问：状态服务是否应该部署在 EKS 中？

默认优先使用 AWS 托管服务。只有当团队具备备份、恢复、升级、监控和故障处理能力时，才考虑在集群内运行状态服务。

docs.title: Capability Map
docs.text:
DevOps is not "someone who writes scripts" and it is not "operations with Kubernetes". It is an engineering capability system that connects software delivery, production operations, reliability, security governance, and organizational feedback.

This site is not a tool directory. It is organized the way a senior DevOps engineer looks at production systems: how systems are designed, how change is delivered, how failure is recovered, how problems become visible, how capability is platformed, and how experience is reviewed.

This section is the site's constitution. It answers three questions before you decide what to read next:

Page Question it answers DevOps Capability Topology Which layered capabilities make up DevOps? Value and Maturity How is DevOps value defined and maturity rated? Self-Assessment and Growth Path Am I Ops, junior DevOps, or close to architect or expert? Content Maturity Map Which sections are mature, which are under construction, what should I read first?

After this section, you should be able to judge whether this site is valuable to you, and which content is most worth reading first.

docs.title: Content Maturity Map
docs.text: This site is under construction. Instead of using empty sections to fake completeness, it tells you the real status of each area and what is most worth reading first.

docs.title: Maturity Legend
docs.text:
• Mature: Credible, directly usable content already exists.
  • Reference: Operational standards, good for looking up exact steps, but not a substitute for deep articles.
  • Flagship pending: The section frame exists, but the deep flagship article is not yet written.
  • Under construction: Only a positioning statement exists; content is still being accumulated.

docs.title: Section Status
docs.text: Section Status What you can read now Capability Map Mature Capability topology, value and maturity, self-assessment matrix — the site's judgment framework System Design Mature (flagship) AWS EKS Production HA Cluster Standard is the site quality benchmark; plus 5 Kubernetes production standards Change Delivery Reference Argo CD GitOps, Jenkins→K8s pipeline, deployment rollback runbook Reliable Operations Reference 6 observability standards + 4 recovery runbooks (PVC, disk expansion, cert expiry, health check) Platform Governance Reference GitLab, JumpServer bastion, publication safety checklist, Redash Cases & Reviews Flagship pending 3 anonymous migration cases exist; deep dives and decision reviews are under construction Standards Manual Mature (largest) ~30 operational standards organized by technical domain + document templates, ready for lookup

docs.title: Three Recommended Reading Paths
docs.text:
1. To judge whether this site is serious: read the AWS EKS Production HA Cluster Standard directly — it is the site quality benchmark.
  2. To position yourself: read Self-Assessment and Growth Path and score yourself on the six-dimension matrix.
  3. To look up a tool's standard practice: go to the Standards Manual and search by technical domain or tag.

Flagship-pending sections will not waste your time with empty writing-rule pages — they are clearly marked "under construction" and link to the nearest real content.

docs.title: Value and Maturity
docs.text:
Definition: DevOps is an engineering collaboration model that uses automation, standardization, observability, and feedback loops to connect development delivery and production operations into a continuous improvement system.

Its value is not measured by the number of tools used. It is measured by whether this loop keeps improving:

flowchart LR A["Smaller changes"] --> B["Faster delivery"] B --> C["Lower failure rate"] C --> D["Faster recovery"] D --> E["More trustworthy business cadence"] E --> A Value Dimension Low Maturity High Maturity Quantitative Signals Delivery Speed Manual releases and remembered steps Standardized pipelines and clear environment promotion Deployment frequency, lead time Change Safety Release depends on experience; failures are handled ad hoc Canary, rollback, freeze windows, release gates Change failure rate, rollback time Recovery Capability Incidents depend on individual heroics SLOs, alert tiers, drills, and reviews MTTR, incident impact duration System Transparency Monitoring exists but diagnosis is slow Metrics, logs, traces, and business context are connected MTTD, alert usefulness Cost Efficiency Idle resources have no ownership Capacity models, cost attribution, elasticity strategy Unit business cost, idle resource ratio Security Governance Secrets sprawl and scanning happens late Secrets, audit, supply chain, and security gates shift left Critical vulnerability repair time, permission drift count

docs.title: Five Maturity Levels
docs.text:
Level State Characteristics Main Risk L1 Tooling Scripts and tools exist Repetition is reduced, but capability depends on individuals Automating the wrong process L2 Process Standard processes exist Release, rollback, approval, and troubleshooting become reusable Process exists but execution quality varies L3 Platform Shared capabilities exist Multiple teams use common templates and platforms Platform becomes a new ticket queue L4 Measured Engineering metrics exist Delivery, reliability, cost, and security can be evaluated with data Metrics replace judgment L5 Adaptive Feedback loops exist Incidents, cost, and delivery data improve platforms and processes Organizational complexity requires governance

Real DevOps maturity is not "having a platform". It is whether platforms, processes, and team behavior continuously reduce production risk.

Once you know your position, score yourself with the Self-Assessment and Growth Path.

docs.title: Self-Assessment and Growth Path
docs.text: Start by separating role boundaries. Not all operations work is DevOps, and not all DevOps work requires building a platform. The key question is whether you can connect system operations with change delivery and improve both through engineering methods.

docs.title: Level Bands
docs.text: Level Common State What You Can Do Independently What Is Still Missing Not DevOps Only uses applications or follows simple deployment steps Operate a few tools by documentation Missing systems, networking, release, and incident foundations Below Ops Can run commands but cannot judge blast radius Restart services, copy files, read simple logs Missing Linux, networking, permissions, backup, and recovery capability Ops Can maintain production systems Hosts, services, logs, backups, routine incidents Missing delivery automation, platform thinking, and reliability design Junior DevOps Can script repeated work Basic CI/CD, container builds, simple deployment Missing production risk awareness and rollback design Mid-Level DevOps Can own a delivery path Pipelines, Kubernetes deployment, monitoring, routine troubleshooting Missing cross-team standards, capacity, security, and cost governance Senior DevOps Can design production-grade solutions HA architecture, canary release, SLOs, incident reviews, platform templates Missing organization-level trade-off and long-term evolution capability DevOps Architect Can define multi-team standards Platform architecture, governance model, golden paths, migration strategy Needs stronger business constraint, cost, and organizational impact judgment DevOps Expert Can handle complex systems and organizational problems Identify wrong paths, build feedback loops, use reviews to upgrade the system Risk is leaving frontline details and only speaking in principles

docs.title: Self-Scoring Matrix
docs.text:
Score each dimension from 0 to 4:

  • 0: You do not understand it and cannot judge independently.
  • 1: You can follow documentation.
  • 2: You can deliver routine work independently.
  • 3: You can design solutions and handle exceptions.
  • 4: You can define standards, guide others, and improve with data.
Dimension 0-1 Points 2 Points 3-4 Points Linux / Network / Storage Only common commands Can troubleshoot common system issues Can design capacity, isolation, recovery, and security baselines Containers / Kubernetes / Cloud Can deploy examples Can maintain routine clusters and services Can design production HA, upgrade, and recovery strategies CI/CD / Artifacts / Environments Can run a pipeline Can maintain release flow Can design safe, reversible, auditable delivery systems Observability / Incident Handling Can read logs and dashboards Can diagnose common incidents Can design alert tiers, SLOs, and review loops Security / Compliance / Cost Knows basic concepts Can run scans and configure permissions Can embed governance into platform and delivery flow Platform / Standardization / Influence Serves only yourself Can package scripts and templates Can serve multiple teams and quantify platform value

docs.title: Score Interpretation
docs.text:
Total Score Judgment Recommendation 0-6 Below Ops or just starting Build Linux, networking, permissions, logging, backup, and incident foundations first 7-12 Ops to junior DevOps Script repeated work and build basic CI/CD and containerization capability 13-18 Mid-level DevOps Focus on reliability, rollback, observability, and shift-left security 19-22 Senior DevOps Work on cross-team standards, platform abstraction, capacity, and cost governance 23-24 Architect / expert candidate Prove system-level impact with real cases, incident reviews, and metrics

Do not judge progress by how many tools you have learned. Better evidence is whether you made releases safer, recovery faster, incidents rarer, cost clearer, and teams less dependent on individual memory.

Once you know your band, use the Content Maturity Map to find what to read next.

docs.title: DevOps Capability Topology
docs.text:
DevOps capability is not a tool list. It is a layered capability stack from infrastructure to organizational feedback. Tools matter only when they produce verifiable engineering outcomes.

flowchart TB A["Foundation<br/>Linux / Network / Storage / Security baseline"] --> B["Runtime<br/>Containers / Kubernetes / Cloud resources / Middleware"] B --> C["Delivery<br/>Git / CI / CD / Artifacts / Environment governance"] C --> D["Reliability<br/>SLO / Capacity / Change risk / DR / Rollback"] D --> E["Observability<br/>Metrics / Logs / Traces / Alerting / Reviews"] E --> F["Platform<br/>Self-service / Standardization / Reuse / Cost governance"] F --> G["Organizational Feedback<br/>Metrics / Ownership / Decision reviews / Engineering culture"] A -.supports.-> D C -.constrains.-> D E -.feeds back.-> C G -.improves.-> F Capability Domain Core Question Typical Artifacts Evidence Of Capability Infrastructure Can the system be installed, hardened, and recovered? Baseline images, network plans, storage policies, access controls You can explain how a host or network failure is diagnosed and recovered Containers And Cloud Native Can services be portable, schedulable, and scalable? Dockerfiles, Helm charts, Kubernetes clusters, cloud modules You can design production release, scaling, rollback, and isolation strategies Delivery Engineering Are changes repeatable, auditable, and reversible? CI/CD pipelines, artifact repositories, environment promotion, release gates You reduce release failure rate, not just automate commands Reliability Engineering Does failure have boundaries, playbooks, and recovery paths? SLOs, capacity models, degradation plans, drills, incident reviews You turn incidents into structural improvements Observability Can teams locate problems quickly instead of only "having monitoring"? Metrics, logs, traces, alert tiers, on-call dashboards You reduce alert noise and improve diagnosis speed Platform Engineering Can multiple teams deliver and operate through shared standards? Self-service platform, golden paths, templates, permission models, cost reports Teams need fewer tickets, use more self-service, and make fewer repeated mistakes Security And Governance Is speed built on controlled risk? Secrets management, audit, supply chain scanning, least privilege, compliance checks Security is moved into delivery flow instead of patched before release

A reliable DevOps engineer does not need to be the strongest in every layer, but must understand how the layers depend on each other. Tool-only knowledge without delivery risk, delivery without production reliability, or platform work without team feedback will all fail under production pressure.

This topology is also the source of the site navigation: System Design covers foundation and runtime, Change Delivery covers the delivery layer, Reliable Operations covers reliability and observability, and Platform Governance covers the platform and organizational feedback layers.

docs.title: Cases & Reviews
docs.text:
Cases and reviews prove experience. Deep dives, case studies, decision reviews, and incident reviews record the judgment that only someone who has actually done the work can write.

This content proves experience more strongly than success stories. It preserves scale, constraints, symptoms, trade-offs, and outcomes, but removes identifying information.

docs.title: Anonymization Boundary
docs.text:
• Can retain: service scale, node count ranges, failure symptoms, migration phases, constraints, decision reasons, and outcome ranges.
  • Must remove: customer names, company names, real domains, accounts, secrets, private repositories, internal IPs, personal data, and non-public business details.

docs.text:

  • Decision Reviews: how a wrong technology decision was discovered and corrected.
  • Incident Reviews: timeline, symptoms vs root cause, misjudgment, structural fixes.

These flagship articles are under construction and will be written once enough real, anonymized material exists. See the Content Maturity Map.

docs.title: Monitoring Migration Is a Routing Migration, Not a Server Migration
Core Claim
docs.text:
A monitoring migration — moving or rebuilding a Prometheus, Alertmanager, Grafana, and notification stack — is mostly a migration of labels, alert rules, and alert routing, not a migration of binaries.

The binaries are the easy part. Teams reliably get the new Prometheus, Alertmanager, and Grafana running. What breaks is subtler: alerts that silently never route, dashboards that point at the wrong data source, scrape labels that changed so old rules no longer match, and notification templates that either go quiet or leak sensitive data. The system looks migrated because the UIs load, but its job — telling humans when production is unhealthy — has quietly regressed.

docs.title: Why It Is Misunderstood
docs.text:
The migration is planned as an infrastructure task ("stand up the new servers, point things at them, decommission the old ones"), so success is measured by "the new stack is up." That is the wrong success criterion.

The real success criterion is: a real failure still produces the right alert to the right people. That property lives in labels, rule expressions, routing trees, and receiver secrets — none of which are validated by checking that pods are Ready.

Two forces make this worse:

  • Alert rules and routing are usually owned by different people than the platform doing the migration, so continuity assumptions are never written down.
  • The failure is invisible until an incident. A monitoring stack that has stopped alerting looks identical to a healthy quiet system.

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
Any monitoring migration operates under constraints that shape the plan:

  • The old stack must stay authoritative until the new one is proven, because you cannot detect a gap with the same system you are migrating.
  • Scrape target labels (job, instance, namespace, app, and application-specific labels) are part of the alerting contract. Changing them changes which alerts fire.
  • Alert rules and Alertmanager routing must be deployed together and version-controlled together; a rule with no matching route is silent.
  • Notification receivers carry secrets (webhook URLs, SMTP credentials, chat tokens) that must move as secrets, not as copied config.

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
A migration validated only by "the stack is up" repeatedly produces the same outcomes:

  • An alert that fired daily on the old stack is never seen again on the new one, because a relabel step renamed the label its expression matched on.
  • A dashboard shows data, but from the old data source, so the team believes the new Prometheus is scraping when it is not.
  • A receiver was copied without its secret, so Alertmanager logs delivery failures that nobody reads.
  • A notification template that once included raw request context is carried over and now leaks that context into a chat channel.

The common thread: every one of these passes a "does the UI work" check and fails a "does a real failure alert" check.

docs.title: Migration Checklist
docs.text:
Treat this as the migration's real definition of done:

  • Existing dashboards still answer real operational questions, not just render panels.
  • Prometheus scrape targets and labels are preserved, or changed intentionally with the dependent rules updated in the same change.
  • Alert rules and Alertmanager routing are deployed together from version control.
  • Notification templates do not leak secrets, request bodies, or personal data.
  • Webhook and SMTP endpoints are stored as secrets or platform-managed values, never inline.
  • Application exporters expose the expected application label that rules depend on.
  • The rollback path keeps the old monitoring stack running and authoritative through the observation window.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• Prometheus target count on the new stack matches the expected inventory, not just "more than zero."
  • All alert rules load without syntax errors.
  • A synthetic alert traverses the full path and reaches the real notification channel.
  • Grafana dashboards are confirmed to use the new data source.
  • A controlled "application down" scenario produces the expected alert with the expected routing — the single most important test.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Cause Silent alert gap A known alert never fires after migration Relabeling changed a label the rule expression matched on Wrong data source Dashboards render but show stale or no data Panels still bound to the old Prometheus Dead receiver Alertmanager logs delivery errors Receiver copied without its secret Routing drop Rule fires but no human is notified Rule has no matching route in the new routing tree Template leak Sensitive data appears in a notification channel Notification template carried over unredacted

docs.title: Counterexamples
docs.text:
This claim does not mean every monitoring change is high risk:

  • Adding a new dashboard or a new exporter alongside an unchanged alerting path is a low-risk additive change, not a migration.
  • Upgrading the Prometheus binary in place, with labels, rules, and routing untouched, is genuinely an infrastructure task and can be validated as one.
  • A fully GitOps-managed stack where rules, routing, and dashboards already live in version control reduces this to a controlled apply — the routing migration risk was paid down earlier.

The risk concentrates specifically when ownership, labels, or routing change at the same time as the servers.

docs.title: Practical Judgment
docs.text:
• Plan the migration around alert routing continuity, and make "a real failure still alerts the right people" the acceptance test.
  • Run old and new stacks in parallel; let the old one stay authoritative until the new one passes a live failure test.
  • Version-control rules and routing together and migrate them as one change.
  • Treat notification secrets and templates as a security review item, not a copy step.

docs.title: Platform Governance
docs.text:
Platform engineering turns repeated delivery, operation, security, and governance work into reusable platform capabilities. It is not wrapping scripts in a UI.

Platform articles must distinguish platform capability from platform interface. If a feature does not reduce cognitive load, repeated risk, or feedback time, it may not deserve to be platformized.

Security is also not an isolated checklist; it is part of the platform and delivery system. Least privilege, credential lifecycle, supply chain risk, audit, and change control should be embedded in DevOps workflows.

docs.title: Reliable Operations
docs.text:
Reliable operations is not a collection of monitoring tools. It manages system failure through objectives, signals, response, drills, and review.

The core idea of observability is not "do we have monitoring" but "can the system explain how it broke". Metrics, logs, traces, and events should serve diagnosis, not dashboard count.

docs.title: Trigger
docs.text:
Use this runbook when you need to add OpenTelemetry traces, and optionally metrics and logs, to a JVM service that runs outside Kubernetes auto-instrumentation — for example a Docker or VM-hosted Java service exporting to an OTLP backend such as SigNoz.

For Kubernetes workloads, prefer the OpenTelemetry Auto-Instrumentation Standard instead of attaching the agent by hand.

docs.title: Preconditions
docs.text:
• A reachable OTLP-compatible backend or collector endpoint exists.
  • The target is a non-critical Java service for the first run, with a known rollback owner.
  • You have a baseline of startup time, latency, error rate, and memory before attaching the agent.
  • The OpenTelemetry Java agent version is chosen and pinned, downloaded only from the official OpenTelemetry release source.
  • A generic, documentation-safe service name is agreed for the example (for example example-service).

docs.title: Impact Check
docs.text:
• Attaching the agent changes JVM startup and adds memory and CPU overhead. Treat it as a change, not a passive setting.
  • Exported telemetry leaves the process. Confirm where it goes before enabling logs export.
  • Keep application runtime JVM options separate from observability options so a telemetry change cannot silently alter heap or GC behavior.

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. If using a self-hosted backend, deploy it with a pinned version and remove any bundled sample applications before treating the environment as a real test target. Bundled demo data otherwise pollutes service maps.
  2. Place the pinned agent jar at a controlled path, for example /opt/otel/opentelemetry-javaagent.jar.
  3. Configure the agent through environment variables rather than baking values into the image:
export JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS="-javaagent:/opt/otel/opentelemetry-javaagent.jar"
export OTEL_SERVICE_NAME="example-service"
export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT="http://otel-collector.example.internal:4317"
export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_PROTOCOL="grpc"
export OTEL_RESOURCE_ATTRIBUTES="service.namespace=example-namespace,deployment.environment=staging,service.version=1.0.0"
export OTEL_TRACES_EXPORTER="otlp"
export OTEL_METRICS_EXPORTER="none"
export OTEL_LOGS_EXPORTER="none"

JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS is read by every JVM started in that environment, not just the target service. On a shared host this can attach the agent to unintended JVMs. For a single service, prefer adding -javaagent:/opt/otel/opentelemetry-javaagent.jar directly to that service's launch command instead of exporting JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS globally. 4. Set service.name through OTEL_SERVICE_NAME or resource attributes — never leave it as the default unknown_service:java. 5. Enable metrics and logs export only intentionally. Start with traces only; turn on OTEL_METRICS_EXPORTER and OTEL_LOGS_EXPORTER as separate, deliberate steps. 6. Start the Java service and generate a known test request.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• The Java process starts with the agent attached and no startup error in the application log.
  • The backend shows the service under the agreed generic service.name, not a leftover production name and not unknown_service:java.
  • Traces appear after the test request, within the agreed time window. Expand the UI time range when first validating.
  • Metrics appear only if the metrics exporter was deliberately enabled.
  • Logs export, if enabled, contains no credentials, tokens, request bodies, or personal data.
  • Startup time, latency, and memory remain within the agreed threshold versus the baseline.

docs.title: Rollback or Escalation
docs.text:
1. Remove the -javaagent option from JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS (or the launch command) and restart the service.
  2. Confirm the process starts cleanly without the agent and returns to its baseline.
  3. Keep the previous launch configuration until the observation window passes.
  4. Escalate to the backend or platform owner if traces never arrive despite a reachable endpoint and a correctly attached agent — the issue is usually endpoint, protocol, or collector-side, not the application.

docs.title: Post-Check
docs.text:
• Endpoint uses a stable internal address, not a hardcoded internal IP or a developer machine.
  • The example and any committed config use placeholders only.
  • The service name and resource attributes are safe to expose and free of customer or tenant identifiers.

docs.title: Loki Binary Deployment and Alerting Standard
Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to run Loki as a binary systemd service, with Alertmanager-based alerting, in environments where a full Kubernetes Helm deployment is not appropriate.

It complements the Loki Logging Standard, which covers label design, retention principles, and Kubernetes collection. This page covers the operational boundary specific to a non-Helm install: service isolation, lifecycle, ruler configuration, and keeping notification secrets out of published configuration.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • You run a small or single-node environment where a binary install is simpler to operate than Helm.
  • You need log-based alerting through the Loki ruler and Alertmanager.
  • You want a controlled, reproducible systemd service rather than an ad hoc process.

Prefer the Helm deployment model (see the Loki Logging Standard) for multi-tenant, scalable, or Kubernetes-native environments.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • Loki label and cardinality design (see the Loki Logging Standard).
  • Microservice-mode or simple-scalable Loki topologies.
  • Grafana dashboard design.
  • Notification provider internals beyond keeping their credentials out of config.

docs.title: Service isolation
docs.text:
• Run Loki and Alertmanager under a dedicated, non-login service user, not as root and not as a human account.
  • Keep binaries under a controlled, version-pinned install path.
  • Separate the data, configuration, and rule directories so retention, backup, and permission rules can differ per directory.
  • Manage the lifecycle with systemd units, not with nohup or a terminal session.

Example directory layout:

/opt/loki/bin/            # pinned binaries
/etc/loki/                # configuration
/etc/loki/rules/          # ruler rule groups
/var/lib/loki/            # chunk and index data

docs.title: Retention and ingestion limits
docs.text:
• Set retention explicitly and validate it against the data disk capacity before enabling.
  • Set ingestion rate and burst limits so a misbehaving log source cannot fill the disk.
  • Keep compactor retention enabled when you rely on retention for cost control; retention settings without a working compactor do not actually delete data.

docs.title: Ruler and Alertmanager
docs.text:
• Configure the Loki ruler to load rule groups from /etc/loki/rules/.
  • Point the ruler at an internal Alertmanager address only. Use a generic placeholder in any published example:
ruler:
  alertmanager_url: http://alertmanager.example.internal:9093
  storage:
    type: local
    local:
      directory: /etc/loki/rules

The Alertmanager v2 API is the default on current Loki, so no extra flag is needed. On much older Loki versions you may still need to opt in explicitly; verify against the version you run.

  • Keep alert routing in Alertmanager, not embedded in each rule.

docs.title: Keep notification secrets out of configuration
docs.text:
This is the highest-risk part of a binary alerting stack.

  • Never embed webhook URLs, bot tokens, chat IDs, or SMTP credentials in Loki, ruler, Alertmanager, or Grafana configuration that could be committed or published.
  • Load notification secrets from a separate file referenced by environment, a secret store, or systemd credentials — not from the tracked config.
  • In any documentation or example, use placeholders only: https://hooks.example.com/services/REDACTED, smtp.example.com, alerts@example.com.

Example Alertmanager receiver using an environment-provided secret rather than an inline value:

receivers:
  - name: platform-oncall
    webhook_configs:
      - url: ${ALERT_WEBHOOK_URL}   # provided via systemd EnvironmentFile, not committed

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
1. systemctl status loki and systemctl status alertmanager report active services with no restart loop.
  2. The Loki HTTP endpoint responds locally: 
    curl -s http://127.0.0.1:3100/ready
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:3100/metrics | head
  3. The ruler loads a test rule group without parse errors (check /loki/api/v1/rules and service logs).
  4. A synthetic alert reaches Alertmanager through a non-secret placeholder receiver during the test.
  5. Grafana queries Loki through the internal service address; the Loki port is not exposed publicly.
  6. Disk usage trend after enabling retention matches the expected steady state.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Cause Correct response Secret in config Webhook or SMTP credential visible in tracked config Notification credentials embedded inline Move to EnvironmentFile or secret store; rotate the exposed credential. Disk fills Loki stops ingesting or the host runs out of space Retention set without validating disk capacity, or compactor disabled Lower retention or expand disk; confirm compactor is running. Silent ruler Rules present but no alerts fire Ruler not pointed at Alertmanager, or rule parse error Check ruler logs and alertmanager_url; validate rule syntax. Public Loki port Loki API reachable from outside the trust boundary Service bound to a public interface Bind to internal interface; place access behind the platform proxy. Log volume spike Sudden storage pressure Debug logging left enabled Set log level back to info or warn before steady-state operation.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
1. Stop the affected service with systemd; the unit file makes start and stop deterministic.
  2. Restore the previous configuration and rule directory from backup.
  3. Restart and re-run the validation steps.
  4. If a secret was exposed, rotate it regardless of rollback success.

Keep the previous configuration and binary version until the observation window passes.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Notification secrets must never live in tracked or published configuration.
  • The Loki API must stay inside the trust boundary; do not expose it directly to the internet.
  • Run services under a dedicated low-privilege user.
  • Do not log credentials, tokens, or full authorization headers into Loki itself.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the platform or observability team that runs the binary stack.
  • Re-validate retention against disk capacity whenever log volume grows materially.
  • Re-confirm that no notification secret has drifted into tracked configuration on each change.
  • Re-check binary and ruler compatibility on each Loki upgrade.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Loki Logging Standard
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard
  • Publication Safety Checklist

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard defines a safe baseline for collecting Kubernetes application logs with Loki-compatible tooling.

docs.title: Logging Principles
docs.text:
1. Logs should support troubleshooting without exposing secrets.
  2. Labels should be low-cardinality.
  3. Retention should match compliance and cost requirements.
  4. Application logs should use structured fields where possible.
  5. Sensitive fields must be filtered before storage.

docs.title: Label Guidance
docs.text:
Recommended labels:

cluster
namespace
pod
container
app
environment
team

Avoid labels such as request ID, user ID, email, order ID, or full URL because they create high cardinality or privacy risk.

docs.title: Kubernetes Collection Flow
docs.text:
1. Deploy log collector as a DaemonSet.
  2. Collect container logs from node log paths.
  3. Add Kubernetes metadata.
  4. Drop sensitive or high-cardinality labels.
  5. Send logs to Loki.
  6. Validate queries in Grafana.

docs.title: Deployment Models
docs.text:
Choose the deployment model deliberately; it changes retention, scaling, and access design.

Model Use when Note Single binary / monolithic Small or single-tenant environments Simplest to operate; see the Loki Binary Deployment and Alerting Standard for the non-Helm systemd variant. Simple scalable Moderate volume, want read/write separation Good default for most Kubernetes installs. Microservices High volume, multi-tenant Highest operational cost; only when scale requires it.

docs.title: Helm deployment sequence
docs.text:
1. Add and pin the Helm chart repository and chart version. Do not track latest.
  2. Install or upgrade Loki with an explicit values file under version control.
  3. Install Grafana only if no platform Grafana already exists; otherwise reuse it.
  4. Configure the Grafana data source using the in-cluster service name, not a kubectl port-forward address.
  5. Confirm the collection agent (DaemonSet) is shipping logs before querying by label.

docs.title: Deployment validation
docs.text:
• Loki pods are Ready and the gateway or query frontend is reachable from Grafana.
  • The collection agent targets are healthy.
  • A known pod emits logs and appears in LogQL.
  • The retention setting matches the agreed operational requirement.

docs.title: Deployment failure modes
docs.text: Failure mode Cause port-forward treated as production access Using a developer convenience as an access pattern Storage pressure later Deploying with default retention and never validating disk capacity Unqueryable logs Missing the labels needed for useful queries Duplicate Grafana Installing a new Grafana when a platform Grafana already exists

docs.title: Validation Queries
docs.text:
{namespace="example-namespace"}
{app="example-service"} |= "error"

docs.title: Retention and Cost
docs.text:
Define:

  • Default retention.
  • Extended retention for critical services.
  • Excluded namespaces.
  • Log volume budget.
  • Query performance expectations.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Do not log credentials, tokens, session IDs, or full authorization headers.
  • Mask personal data at application or collector layer.
  • Restrict access to logs by team and environment.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Loki Binary Deployment and Alerting Standard
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard
  • Publication Safety Checklist

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to enable OpenTelemetry auto-instrumentation on Kubernetes workloads without treating it as a blind namespace-wide toggle.

OpenTelemetry auto-instrumentation is implemented through the OpenTelemetry Operator and Kubernetes admission mutation. It can add init containers, agents, environment variables, and runtime configuration to new pods. That means it changes application startup, memory use, network egress, and the telemetry data leaving the cluster.

The core judgment of this standard:

Auto-instrumentation is a production change, not an observability toggle. Roll it out one workload at a time, with validation and rollback, the same way you would ship any change that mutates a running pod.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • You run the OpenTelemetry Operator, or plan to install it, on Kubernetes.
  • You want traces, and optionally metrics or logs, from services that cannot be manually instrumented yet.
  • You need consistent runtime agent configuration across multiple services.
  • You want a safe bridge from "no traces" to "manual instrumentation for critical paths".

This is most useful for platform, SRE, and DevOps teams that own observability enablement but do not own every application codebase.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • Manual SDK instrumentation inside application code.
  • Designing the trace backend itself. See SigNoz Observability Standard for backend selection.
  • Full sampling strategy design beyond the rollout-safe baseline below.
  • Non-Kubernetes agents for virtual machines or bare metal.
  • Vendor-specific OpenTelemetry distributions.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before enabling injection, confirm:

  • A reachable OTLP-compatible collector or backend exists.
  • The OpenTelemetry Operator and its admission webhook are installed and healthy.
  • The target namespace has an Instrumentation resource before the target application pod is created.
  • The first target workload is non-critical and has a known rollback owner.
  • The workload has a latency, error-rate, restart, and resource baseline from before injection.
  • The runtime language is known: Java, Node.js, Python, .NET, Go, Deno, or SDK-only injection.

This standard focuses on application runtime instrumentation. Apache HTTPD and NGINX module injection need a separate webserver-module review because their compatibility and configuration risks are different from application agents.

Do not start with a high-traffic, latency-sensitive, or incident-prone service. Auto-instrumentation is safer as a staged rollout than as a platform-wide campaign.

docs.title: Use a collector as the default boundary
docs.text:
Send application telemetry to an in-cluster OpenTelemetry Collector unless there is a deliberate exception.

The collector gives the platform team one place to control:

  • Backend credentials.
  • Retries and queue limits.
  • Memory limiting.
  • Attribute normalization.
  • Filtering of unsafe span, metric, and log attributes.
  • Export routing to one or more backends.

Do not point workloads at localhost, a kubectl port-forward, a node IP, or a developer laptop endpoint. A production Instrumentation resource must reference a stable service name.

Example endpoint pattern:

http://otel-collector.observability.svc.cluster.local:4318

Use the fully qualified service name when the collector is in a different namespace from the application.

docs.title: Set the OTLP protocol explicitly; match the collector port
docs.text:
Do not rely on a per-language default OTLP protocol. The default protocol for each auto-instrumentation runtime is version-dependent and has changed across both the language SDKs and the Operator. Treating "Node.js uses gRPC, everything else uses HTTP" as a fixed rule will eventually point a workload at a receiver port the collector is not listening on, and traces will silently disappear.

The reliable model is a three-part contract you set deliberately, not infer:

  1. Protocol — set OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_PROTOCOL explicitly in the runtime section of the Instrumentation resource (grpc, http/protobuf, or http/json).
  2. Endpoint port — must match the chosen protocol:
OTLP protocol Collector receiver port Endpoint example grpc 4317 http://otel-collector.observability.svc.cluster.local:4317 http/protobuf or http/json 4318 http://otel-collector.observability.svc.cluster.local:4318
  1. Collector receiver — the collector must expose the matching OTLP receiver (gRPC on 4317, HTTP on 4318) for the port you chose.

The http:// scheme on the endpoint is correct for both protocols here; it is the value the agent reads from OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT. What must agree is protocol ↔ port ↔ collector receiver, not the scheme.

Runtime-specific notes that still matter:

  • Python: older Operator versions may require OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_PROTOCOL=http/protobuf explicitly; verify during upgrade.
  • .NET: check the runtime identifier for musl-based images.
  • Go: feature-gated, needs OTEL_GO_AUTO_TARGET_EXE, uses an eBPF sidecar, and does not support multi-container pods (see the Go security exception below).
  • Deno: support may require runtime flags; do not treat it as a stable default without testing.
  • SDK-only: protocol and endpoint come from the SDK configuration you inject; match the collector receiver to whatever you set.

Whenever the Operator version is upgraded, re-confirm the injected protocol against the receiver you run. The protocol is a value you set, not an architecture boundary you inherit.

docs.title: Keep injection opt-in
docs.text:
Use per-workload pod template annotations. Do not use broad namespace-wide injection as the default operating model.

For a Deployment, the annotation belongs under:

spec.template.metadata.annotations

A metadata annotation on the Deployment object itself does not mutate pods.

Language-specific opt-in annotations:

Runtime Injection annotation Java instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-java: "true" Node.js instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-nodejs: "true" Python instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-python: "true" .NET instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-dotnet: "true" Go instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-go: "true" Deno or SDK-only instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-sdk: "true"

Use the Instrumentation resource name instead of "true" when the namespace contains more than one Instrumentation object:

instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-java: "java-standard"

docs.title: Target the container explicitly
docs.text:
Pods with more than one container need explicit targeting.

For a single language instrumentation, use:

instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/container-names: "example-app"

For multiple languages in the same pod, the Operator requires the multi-instrumentation feature flag and language-specific container name annotations:

instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-java: "true"
instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/java-container-names: "example-api"
instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-python: "true"
instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/python-container-names: "example-worker"

Do not let the webhook guess the target container in multi-container pods. It creates a common failure mode: the pod is healthy, the annotation exists, but the service you care about produces no useful telemetry.

docs.title: Keep the first Instrumentation resource minimal
docs.text:
Use one Instrumentation resource per runtime and namespace until the operating model is stable.

Example Java baseline:

apiVersion: opentelemetry.io/v1alpha1
kind: Instrumentation
metadata:
  name: java-standard
  namespace: example-namespace
spec:
  exporter:
    endpoint: http://otel-collector.observability.svc.cluster.local:4318
  propagators:
    - tracecontext
    - baggage
  sampler:
    type: parentbased_traceidratio
    argument: "0.05"
  java:
    env:
      # Set the protocol explicitly so it matches the 4318 HTTP receiver above.
      - name: OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_PROTOCOL
        value: http/protobuf

This example intentionally starts with a low sampling ratio. A sampling value of "1" traces all traffic and is unsafe as a default for high-throughput services.

The protocol is set in the runtime-specific section (spec.java.env here) rather than left to the SDK default, so the 4318 endpoint, the http/protobuf protocol, and the collector's HTTP receiver all agree. Use spec.nodejs.env, spec.python.env, and so on for other runtimes.

Add runtime-specific environment overrides only when they express a deliberate decision, such as disabling a noisy library or setting a compatibility flag. Do not paste a large vendor example into production without reducing it to the behavior you actually want.

docs.title: Treat Go as a security exception
docs.text:
Go auto-instrumentation is different from Java, Python, Node.js, and .NET. It uses eBPF and requires an injected sidecar with elevated privileges. It also requires the target executable path through OTEL_GO_AUTO_TARGET_EXE or the matching annotation.

Do not enable Go auto-instrumentation unless all of these are true:

  • The target pod has only the Go container you intend to instrument.
  • The executable path is known and stable.
  • The security owner accepts the privileged sidecar requirement.
  • A rollback owner is present during the rollout window.

For many Go services, manual instrumentation is the safer long-term standard.

docs.title: Rollout Procedure
docs.text:
1. Select one non-critical workload and one runtime.
  2. Record the baseline: request latency, error rate, pod restart count, CPU, memory, log volume, and current trace volume if any.
  3. Create or update the runtime-specific Instrumentation resource.
  4. If the target is Go, obtain explicit security approval for the privileged sidecar before continuing.
  5. Validate the operator, webhook, collector service, and Instrumentation resource before touching the workload.
  6. Add the injection annotation to the workload pod template.
  7. Let the normal deployment mechanism create new pods, then watch rollout status.
  8. Validate the pod mutation, telemetry path, application health, and backend load.
  9. Wait through an agreed observation window before annotating another workload.

Example workload annotation:

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: example-service
  namespace: example-namespace
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-java: "java-standard"
        instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/container-names: "example-app"

The Instrumentation resource must exist before the application pod is created. If the application pod already exists, the webhook cannot retroactively mutate it. A new pod must be created through a rollout.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Validate in layers and stop at the first failed layer.

docs.title: Layer 1: Operator and webhook
docs.text:
kubectl -n opentelemetry-operator-system get pods
kubectl get mutatingwebhookconfigurations
kubectl -n opentelemetry-operator-system logs -l app.kubernetes.io/name=opentelemetry-operator --container manager --since=10m

Expected result:

  • Operator pods are Ready.
  • The mutating webhook configuration exists.
  • Operator logs do not show injection, certificate, or admission errors for the target namespace.

docs.title: Layer 2: Collector reachability
docs.text:
kubectl -n observability get svc otel-collector
kubectl -n observability get pods -l app.kubernetes.io/name=otel-collector
kubectl -n observability logs deploy/otel-collector --since=10m

Expected result:

  • The collector service exposes the protocol and port required by the target runtime.
  • Collector pods are Ready.
  • Collector logs do not show receiver bind failures, exporter authentication errors, queue saturation, or memory limiter drops during the test window.

docs.title: Layer 3: Instrumentation resource
docs.text:
kubectl -n example-namespace describe otelinst java-standard

Expected result:

  • The endpoint points to the collector service, not localhost.
  • Propagators and sampler are explicit.
  • The runtime-specific section exists when runtime overrides are required.
  • Events are empty or explain a non-blocking condition.

docs.title: Layer 4: Workload mutation
docs.text:
kubectl -n example-namespace rollout status deploy/example-service
kubectl -n example-namespace get events --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp
kubectl -n example-namespace describe pod -l app.kubernetes.io/name=example-service

Expected result:

  • New pods were created after the annotation was applied.
  • Events show the auto-instrumentation init container or injected agent behavior when applicable.
  • The target container, not a sidecar or unrelated init container, received the instrumentation.
  • Pods are Ready without abnormal startup delay.

docs.title: Layer 5: Telemetry and application health
docs.text:
Validate both observability and service behavior:

  • The backend receives spans from example-service within the agreed test window.
  • Trace service name, namespace, environment, and version attributes are usable for filtering.
  • Request latency, error rate, restart count, and memory remain within the rollout threshold.
  • Log volume does not spike because of agent debug output.
  • Collector queue, dropped spans, and backend ingestion latency remain below alert thresholds.

If application health regresses, roll back. Do not "leave it enabled and watch" unless the service owner explicitly accepts the risk.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
Injection happens when pods are created, so rollback must create clean replacement pods.

  1. Remove the injection annotation from the workload pod template.
  2. Remove runtime-specific container-name annotations from the same pod template.
  3. Roll out the workload through the normal deployment mechanism.
  4. Confirm new pods no longer contain the auto-instrumentation init container, injected agent, or injected OpenTelemetry environment variables.
  5. Confirm application latency, error rate, restart count, and memory return to the pre-injection baseline.
  6. Keep the Instrumentation resource until no annotated workload depends on it.

Only remove the Operator or Instrumentation resource after annotated workloads have been cleaned up. Removing the platform component first can turn later pod creation into an admission or startup failure.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Likely cause Correct response Annotation on the wrong object Deployment has an annotation, but new pods are unchanged Annotation placed under metadata.annotations instead of spec.template.metadata.annotations Move annotation to pod template and create new pods. Resource created too late Annotation exists, but injection did not happen Instrumentation was created after the pod was created Create the Instrumentation first, then roll the workload. Collector unreachable Agent logs connection failures and backend has no spans Wrong service name, namespace, port, protocol, or endpoint defaulted to localhost Fix spec.exporter.endpoint; validate collector receiver ports. Wrong runtime annotation Pod mutates incorrectly or not at all Runtime annotation does not match application language Replace with the correct language annotation. Wrong container targeted Pod is healthy, but no useful spans appear Multi-container pod without explicit container targeting Add container-names or language-specific container name annotation. Backend overload Backend latency, dropped spans, or storage spike after rollout Sampling too high or too many workloads enabled at once Lower sampling, pause rollout, and validate collector queue limits. Startup regression Pods take longer to become Ready or crash on startup Agent image/runtime incompatibility or resource pressure Roll back and test the runtime image/version in staging. Sensitive attribute leakage Traces contain tokens, request bodies, headers, user data, or query strings Default instrumentation captured fields that should have been filtered Disable the offending instrumentation or filter at collector and application layers. Go multi-container failure Go workload does not instrument as expected Go auto-instrumentation does not support multi-container pods Do not use Go auto-instrumentation for that pod shape; use manual instrumentation or split workload.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Treat telemetry egress as a data export path. Review destination, authentication, TLS, and retention before broad rollout.
  • Do not capture request bodies, full headers, query strings, authorization headers, cookies, or user identifiers by default.
  • Pin or approve auto-instrumentation images. An injected agent runs inside every annotated pod.
  • Do not enable Go auto-instrumentation without a security exception for its eBPF and privileged sidecar model.
  • Keep backend credentials in the collector, not in each application workload, whenever possible.
  • Limit who can modify Instrumentation resources and workload injection annotations.
  • Apply resource limits to the collector and monitor queue saturation so a backend outage does not turn into node pressure.
  • Use safe resource attributes such as service name, namespace, environment, team, and version; avoid customer, tenant, email, or request-level identifiers.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: platform or observability team.
  • Review on every OpenTelemetry Operator upgrade.
  • Review on every Kubernetes minor upgrade.
  • Review when enabling a new runtime language.
  • Review when trace volume, backend cost, or collector drops exceed the agreed threshold.
  • Revalidate sampling whenever a high-traffic service is added.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• SigNoz Observability Standard
  • Java Application OpenTelemetry Instrumentation Runbook
  • Prometheus Exporter Monitoring Standard
  • Loki Logging Standard
  • OpenTelemetry Operator auto-instrumentation documentation

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard defines how to write, review, and operate Prometheus alerts for public reusable DevOps documentation.

docs.title: Alert Design Principles
docs.text:
1. Alert only on symptoms that need human action.
  2. Use warning and critical levels with different urgency.
  3. Include service, environment, owner, and runbook labels.
  4. Avoid alerting on short transient spikes unless the service requires it.
  5. Every critical alert must have a runbook.
  6. Alert messages must be safe to share and must not include secrets.

docs.title: Recommended Labels
docs.text:
labels:
  severity: warning
  team: platform
  service: example-service
  runbook: kubernetes-pvc-usage-alert-runbook

docs.title: Recommended Annotations
docs.text:
annotations:
  summary: "PVC usage is above warning threshold"
  description: "PVC {{ $labels.persistentvolumeclaim }} in namespace {{ $labels.namespace }} is using {{ $value }} percent of available capacity."
  action: "Check recent growth, confirm owner, and follow the PVC usage runbook."

docs.title: Example Rule: PVC Capacity
docs.text:
groups:
  - name: kubernetes-storage
    rules:
      - alert: KubernetesPVCUsageHigh
        expr: kubelet_volume_stats_used_bytes / kubelet_volume_stats_capacity_bytes * 100 > 85
        for: 15m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: platform
          service: storage
          runbook: kubernetes-pvc-usage-alert-runbook
        annotations:
          summary: "PVC usage is above 85%"
          description: "PVC {{ $labels.persistentvolumeclaim }} in namespace {{ $labels.namespace }} is above 85% usage for 15 minutes."
          action: "Review growth, confirm owner, and prepare cleanup or expansion."
      - alert: KubernetesPVCUsageCritical
        expr: kubelet_volume_stats_used_bytes / kubelet_volume_stats_capacity_bytes * 100 > 90
        for: 10m
        labels:
          severity: critical
          team: platform
          service: storage
          runbook: kubernetes-pvc-usage-alert-runbook
        annotations:
          summary: "PVC usage is above 90%"
          description: "PVC {{ $labels.persistentvolumeclaim }} in namespace {{ $labels.namespace }} is above 90% usage for 10 minutes."
          action: "Execute the PVC usage runbook and notify the service owner."

docs.title: Alert Review Checklist
docs.text:
Before enabling a new alert:

  • The alert has a clear owner.
  • The expression has been tested in Prometheus.
  • The threshold is based on operational risk, not arbitrary values.
  • The for duration filters normal noise.
  • The alert includes a runbook label.
  • The message does not expose secrets, real internal domains, or customer data.
  • The notification route matches severity.
  • The alert has been observed in a staging or test environment when possible.

docs.title: Notification Routing
docs.text:
Use this model:

Severity Route Expected Response info Dashboard or low-priority channel Review during business hours. warning Team notification channel Acknowledge and plan action. critical On-call channel Immediate response.

Public examples should use placeholder channels such as platform-alerts and never include webhook URLs.

docs.title: Common Anti-Patterns
docs.text:
• Alerting on every pod restart without context.
  • Using critical severity for capacity warning.
  • Embedding real hostnames or domains in alert examples.
  • Creating alerts without runbooks.
  • Alerting on metrics that disappear during outages without using absent() where appropriate.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Kubernetes PVC Usage Alert Runbook
  • Monitoring Migration Is a Routing Migration, Not a Server Migration

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard describes when and how to use SigNoz or OpenTelemetry-compatible observability stacks.

docs.title: Coverage
docs.text:
• Metrics.
  • Traces.
  • Logs.
  • Service maps.
  • Application latency and error analysis.

docs.title: Deployment Checklist
docs.text:
• Define retention.
  • Restrict UI access.
  • Configure OpenTelemetry Collector.
  • Avoid sending secrets in spans or logs.
  • Validate ingestion with a test service.

docs.title: Kubernetes Deployment
docs.text:
Decide first what SigNoz is for: a lab tool, a team observability platform, or a production service. The answer sets the storage, access, and rollback requirements.

Before deploying:

  • Confirm the default StorageClass supports volume expansion and uses a suitable reclaim policy if persistent storage will be used.
  • Pin the chart version.
  • Create a dedicated namespace.
  • Review the resource requests and limits for ClickHouse, the collector, Alertmanager, the frontend, and the query service.

docs.title: Deployment validation
docs.text:
• All SigNoz pods are Ready and the ClickHouse cluster is healthy.
  • ClickHouse tables are initialized before the collector starts ingesting.
  • OpenTelemetry collector pods are running and reachable from instrumented applications.
  • The frontend is reached through a controlled access method, not a kubectl port-forward mistaken for production access.
  • A known test workload sends spans or metrics; expand the UI time range when first validating.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
• helm uninstall the release.
  • Delete PVCs only after explicit approval, because they hold telemetry data.
  • Delete the namespace only after confirming no shared resources live in it.
  • Remove instrumentation annotations or agent configuration from applications that pointed at this stack.

docs.title: Known failure modes
docs.text: Failure mode Cause Collector starts before tables exist ClickHouse schema not initialized Storage cannot grow StorageClass lacks expansion or uses an unsuitable reclaim policy No application telemetry Collector endpoint wrong for the instrumented application Exposure mistaken for production Platform reached only through port-forward

docs.text:

docs.title: Trigger
docs.text:
Use this runbook when the TLS certificate for Harbor, or an equivalent self-hosted container registry, must be rotated — on scheduled renewal, before expiry, or after a certificate or key compromise.

For the upstream detection and expiry-tracking workflow, see the TLS Certificate Expiry Runbook.

docs.title: Preconditions
docs.text:
• The maintenance window and rollback owner are agreed.
  • The new certificate, private key, and full chain are available and the formats are confirmed (PEM, correct chain order, key matches certificate).
  • You know where the registry terminates TLS: directly in the registry component, or in a fronting reverse proxy or ingress.
  • A backup location for the current certificate and key is prepared.

docs.title: Impact Check
docs.text:
• TLS rotation interrupts registry availability only during the reload or restart of the TLS-terminating component. Time it for a low-traffic window.
  • A wrong chain or a key that does not match the certificate breaks every docker login, pull, and push until corrected — including image pulls for running deployments that need to scale or restart.

docs.title: Key Permission Rule (Important Correction)
docs.text:
Some older notes suggest making certificate files broadly readable or world-writable to "make it work." Do not do this. A registry private key that is world-readable or world-writable is a credential leak.

The safe rule:

  • The private key must be readable only by the service account or root-controlled process that needs it.
  • Use restrictive permissions: private key 600 (or 640 owned by the service group that reads it), certificate and chain 644.
  • Never use world-writable permissions on any TLS file.
  • Confirm ownership matches the user the registry or proxy runs as.
# Private key: owner read/write only
sudo chmod 600 /etc/harbor/cert/registry.example.com.key
sudo chown harbor:harbor /etc/harbor/cert/registry.example.com.key

# Certificate and chain: world-readable is fine, not writable
sudo chmod 644 /etc/harbor/cert/registry.example.com.crt

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Identify the exact TLS termination point and its certificate and key paths.
  2. Confirm the new files: chain order is leaf → intermediate(s), and the key matches the certificate: 
    openssl x509 -noout -modulus -in registry.example.com.crt | openssl md5
openssl rsa  -noout -modulus -in registry.example.com.key | openssl md5
    The two checksums must match. The -modulus form applies to RSA keys only. For an ECDSA certificate and key, compare the public keys instead: 
    openssl x509 -in registry.example.com.crt -noout -pubkey | openssl md5
openssl pkey -in registry.example.com.key -pubout | openssl md5
  3. Back up the current certificate and key to the prepared backup location before changing anything.
  4. Install the new certificate and key at the expected paths.
  5. Apply the restrictive permissions from the key permission rule above.
  6. Reload or restart only the TLS-terminating component (proxy, ingress, or the registry service), not the entire platform, to limit blast radius.
  7. Proceed to validation before announcing completion.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
1. The served chain is the expected new certificate: 
    openssl s_client -connect registry.example.com:443 -servername registry.example.com </dev/null \
  | openssl x509 -noout -issuer -subject -dates
  2. Login succeeds: 
    docker login registry.example.com
  3. Pull and push tests pass: 
    docker pull registry.example.com/example/app:1.0.0
docker push registry.example.com/example/app:1.0.1
  4. No client reports a trust or chain error, including Kubernetes nodes and CI runners that pull from this registry.

docs.title: Rollback or Escalation
docs.text:
1. Restore the previous certificate and key from the backup taken in step 3.
  2. Reapply the restrictive permissions.
  3. Reload or restart the TLS-terminating component.
  4. Re-run validation against the restored certificate.
  5. Escalate if clients still fail after a confirmed-good chain and matching key — the issue is likely an intermediate certificate missing from the chain or a stale client trust store.

Keep the rollback files until the observation window passes.

docs.title: Post-Check
docs.text:
• Private key permissions are restrictive and ownership matches the running service.
  • No certificate or key material was committed to a repository or pasted into a ticket or chat.
  • The new expiry date is recorded in the certificate tracking workflow.
  • If this rotation followed a compromise, the old key is destroyed after rollback is no longer needed.

docs.title: Related Standards
docs.text:
• Harbor Registry Standard
  • TLS Certificate Expiry Runbook
  • Nginx Reverse Proxy and TLS Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This runbook handles TLS certificate expiry alerts for public endpoints, ingress resources, or reverse proxies.

docs.title: Trigger
docs.text:
Use this runbook when certificate expiry is below the warning or critical threshold.

Recommended thresholds:

Severity Days Remaining Warning 30 Critical 7

docs.title: Initial Triage
docs.text:
Check the remote certificate:

echo | openssl s_client -servername service.example.com -connect service.example.com:443 2>/dev/null \
  | openssl x509 -noout -subject -issuer -dates

For Kubernetes:

kubectl get ingress -A
kubectl get secret example-tls -n example-namespace

Do not print or export private keys during triage.

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text: Symptom Likely Cause Certificate near expiry Renewal job failed or was not configured. Wrong certificate served Ingress or proxy references the wrong secret or file. Browser hostname warning Certificate SAN does not include requested host. Chain warning Intermediate certificate missing or invalid.

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
1. Identify certificate owner.
  2. Confirm renewal method.
  3. Renew certificate through approved CA process.
  4. Update secret or proxy certificate files.
  5. Validate configuration.
  6. Reload ingress controller or proxy if required.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
curl -Iv https://service.example.com

Confirm:

  • Certificate expiry date is extended.
  • Hostname matches.
  • Chain is valid.
  • Application health endpoint works.

docs.title: Closure Criteria
docs.text:
• Certificate is valid past the warning threshold.
  • Monitoring alert has recovered.
  • Renewal process is documented or fixed.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Nginx Reverse Proxy and TLS Standard
  • Harbor Certificate Rotation Runbook
