docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard describes installation, maintenance, cleanup, and recovery practices for Docker hosts.

docs.title: When To Use
docs.text: Use this standard for single-host Docker workloads, development hosts, lightweight internal services, and build machines. For production orchestration, prefer Kubernetes or another managed scheduler.

docs.title: Installation Principles
docs.text:
1. Install Docker from trusted repositories.
  2. Pin versions when reproducibility matters.
  3. Keep Docker data root on a volume with enough capacity.
  4. Configure logging limits before running long-lived containers.
  5. Avoid running business-critical services from unreviewed Compose files.

docs.title: Host Preparation
docs.text:
sudo mkdir -p /etc/docker
sudo mkdir -p /var/lib/docker

Recommended daemon configuration:

{
  "log-driver": "json-file",
  "log-opts": {
    "max-size": "100m",
    "max-file": "3"
  },
  "storage-driver": "overlay2"
}

Apply:

sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable --now docker
docker info

docs.title: Routine Maintenance
docs.text:
Check disk use:

docker system df
df -h /var/lib/docker

List resources:

docker ps -a
docker images
docker volume ls
docker network ls

docs.title: Disk Pressure Cleanup (guarded)
docs.text:
Use when build cache, old images, stopped containers, or unused networks consume disk.

Preconditions:

  • docker system df reviewed to see what is actually reclaimable.
  • Critical images and containers identified.
  • Current deployment rollback images confirmed available locally or in the registry.
  • Volume cleanup explicitly approved or excluded.

Safe cleanup order (least to most risky):

docker container prune -f   # 1. stopped containers
docker network prune -f     # 2. unused networks
docker image prune -f       # 3. dangling images only (does NOT remove tagged images)
docker builder prune -f     # 4. build cache

Reclaiming "old" tagged images requires docker image prune -a, which removes every image not used by a running container — including the rollback images identified in the preconditions. Use it only after confirming those rollback images are available in the registry, and only with explicit approval:

# High risk: removes all unused images, including local rollback images.
# Only after rollback images are confirmed available in the registry.
docker image prune -a

Skip volume cleanup unless the volume ownership and data lifecycle are known. docker volume prune is shown without -f on purpose, so its interactive confirmation stays as a last guard against deleting stateful data:

# High risk: only with explicit approval and known-disposable volumes.
docker volume prune

Validation:

  • docker system df shows the expected reclaimed space.
  • Running containers remain healthy.
  • Rollback images are still available locally or in the registry.
  • The disk alert clears.

Failure modes:

Failure mode Cause Lost fast rollback Removed an image needed for rollback Data loss Pruned a volume holding stateful data Disrupted release Ran cleanup during an active deployment

docs.title: Docker Network Recovery and Reset
docs.text: Symptoms of Docker network issues include containers failing to resolve names, bridge network conflicts, services becoming unreachable after IP range changes, or an incorrect bridge CIDR.

docs.title: Low-risk checks (do these first)
docs.text:
docker network ls
docker network inspect bridge
ip addr
iptables -S

docs.title: Low-risk recovery
docs.text:
1. Stop affected non-critical containers.
  2. Remove only unused Docker networks.
  3. Restart Docker.
  4. Recreate containers from reviewed Compose or deployment files.

docs.title: Guarded bridge reset (higher risk)
docs.text:
Use a full bridge reset only when the bridge is corrupted, conflicts with host routing, or uses an incorrect CIDR.

Preconditions:

  • Maintenance window approved.
  • Running containers identified.
  • /etc/docker/daemon.json backed up.
  • New bridge CIDR chosen and checked against host, VPN, Kubernetes, and cloud ranges.
  • Remote access does not depend on the Docker bridge being reset.

Procedure:

  1. Stop Docker cleanly (systemctl stop docker).
  2. Confirm no critical container is expected to keep running.
  3. Update /etc/docker/daemon.json with the approved bridge CIDR ("bip" or "default-address-pools") and log rotation settings.
  4. Restart Docker (systemctl start docker). Docker rebuilds its default bridge from the new configuration on start; in the normal case you do not delete the bridge by hand.
  5. Only if the stale docker0 interface persists after Docker is stopped and blocks the new CIDR, remove it explicitly while Docker is stopped — never while it is running: 
    sudo ip link set docker0 down
sudo ip link delete docker0
  6. Validate bridge address, container networking, NAT, and service reachability.

Rollback: restore the previous daemon.json from backup and restart Docker.

Do not delete /var/lib/docker as a normal repair step. Data deletion is a separate action that requires its own backup and approval gate; never include it in routine network repair.

docs.title: Compose File Safety
docs.text:
Before running a Compose stack:

  • Review images and tags.
  • Replace hardcoded credentials with environment variables or secret references.
  • Confirm volumes and bind mounts.
  • Confirm exposed ports.
  • Add logging limits.
  • Add restart policies.
  • Document backup and restore for stateful services.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
docker version
docker info
docker run --rm hello-world
docker system df

docs.text:

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard describes safe operation of Harbor or similar private container registries.

docs.title: Core Controls
docs.text:
• Enable authentication.
  • Use project-level permissions.
  • Enable vulnerability scanning where available.
  • Configure retention policies.
  • Back up registry metadata and image storage.
  • Use TLS for all registry traffic.
  • Do not publish robot account tokens.

docs.title: Deployment Checklist
docs.text: Area Check TLS Certificate valid and renewal documented. Storage Object storage or persistent volume backed up. Access Admin account protected, robot accounts scoped. Retention Unused tags cleaned safely. Replication If used, credentials stored securely.

docs.title: Validation

docker login registry.example.com
docker pull registry.example.com/example/app:1.0.0

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard summarizes safe operating practices for Kubernetes API gateways such as APISIX, Kong, or Nginx-based ingress gateways.

docs.title: Gateway Responsibilities
docs.text:
An API gateway commonly handles:

  • Routing.
  • TLS termination.
  • Authentication and authorization integration.
  • Rate limiting.
  • Request and response transformation.
  • Observability.
  • Canary or weighted routing.

docs.title: Deployment Checklist
docs.text:
Before production use:

  • Gateway controller is highly available.
  • Data plane pods have readiness and liveness probes.
  • Admin API is not publicly exposed.
  • Dashboard access is protected.
  • TLS certificate lifecycle is documented.
  • Metrics are scraped by Prometheus.
  • Access logs are routed to the logging platform.
  • Rate limiting and timeout defaults are defined.

docs.title: Route Template
docs.text:
Use placeholders in public examples:

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: example-gateway-route
  namespace: example-namespace
spec:
  ingressClassName: example-gateway
  rules:
    - host: api.example.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /v1
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: example-api
                port:
                  number: 80

docs.title: Security Controls
docs.text:
• Disable anonymous admin access.
  • Restrict admin endpoints by network policy.
  • Store admin keys in a secret manager.
  • Use separate routes for internal and external APIs.
  • Enforce request size and timeout limits.
  • Enable audit logging for route changes.

docs.title: Observability
docs.text:
Minimum metrics:

  • Request rate.
  • Error rate.
  • Latency percentiles.
  • Upstream health.
  • Gateway pod CPU and memory.
  • Route-level 4xx and 5xx counts.

docs.title: Kubernetes Deployment Profile
docs.text:
Treat a Kubernetes API gateway (such as APISIX) as a platform component, not a single application's dependency. Its failure or misconfiguration affects every route behind it.

Decide these points before production traffic:

  • Exposure model: NodePort, LoadBalancer, or ingress-managed.
  • Control-plane datastore topology and persistence (for example etcd members and their storage).
  • StorageClass and reclaim policy for any persistent state.
  • Admin API access boundary.
  • Dashboard authentication and network restriction.
  • Prometheus metric exposure.
  • Resource limits for the gateway and its datastore.

Production judgments:

  • Keep admin APIs internal and network-restricted; never expose them publicly.
  • Avoid wildcard admin allowlists.
  • Do not commit admin tokens or keys into values files; use a secret manager.
  • Pin the chart and gateway versions.
  • Validate datastore (for example etcd) health before routing production traffic.
  • Treat plugin and route configuration as change-managed platform config.

docs.title: Deployment validation
docs.text:
• Datastore members (for example etcd) are healthy.
  • Gateway pods are Ready and the gateway service receives traffic.
  • The admin API is reachable only from allowed network paths.
  • The Prometheus endpoint exposes gateway metrics.
  • A test route can be created, queried, and removed cleanly.

docs.title: Change Procedure
docs.text:
1. Review route manifest.
  2. Validate service and port.
  3. Apply to staging.
  4. Run smoke test.
  5. Apply to production.
  6. Confirm metrics and logs.
  7. Record rollback command.

Rollback:

kubectl -n example-namespace rollout undo deployment/example-api
kubectl -n example-namespace delete ingress example-gateway-route

docs.title: System Design
docs.text:
System design answers one question: can a system operate under real traffic, real failure, and real organizational constraints.

Architecture articles must explain constraints, alternatives, failure modes, validation, and rollback. A component diagram is not enough.

docs.title: Production Architecture
docs.text:

  • Kubernetes Cluster Provisioning Standard
  • Kubernetes Storage and Ingress Standard
  • kubeadm Installation Standard
  • EKS and Kubernetes Upgrade Standard
  • NFS PVC Capacity Management

docs.title: Reading Suggestion
docs.text: If you want one article to judge the depth of this site, read the EKS HA cluster standard. From conclusion-first structure, applicable and non-applicable boundaries, explicit assumptions, failure modes, to rollback paths, it is the quality template for everything else.

docs.title: AWS EKS Production High Availability Cluster Design and Delivery Standard
Executive Summary
docs.text:
Building a production-grade highly available EKS cluster on AWS is not mainly about increasing node count or creating a Kubernetes control plane. The real work is putting control plane access, worker capacity, networking, identity, ingress, storage, delivery, observability, upgrades, and recovery into one verifiable operating model.

A credible production EKS design should provide:

  • Workload placement across multiple Availability Zones.
  • Scheduling continuity when one AZ loses capacity.
  • Recoverable and scalable worker capacity.
  • Workload-level AWS permissions instead of broad node-level credentials.
  • Clear ownership for ingress, DNS, certificates, logs, metrics, and alerts.
  • Validation and rollback paths for each critical change.

If a guide stops at eksctl create cluster, it proves that a cluster can be created. It does not prove that the platform can carry production traffic.

docs.title: When To Use This Standard
docs.text:
Use this document when:

  • Creating a new production Amazon EKS cluster.
  • Migrating workloads from virtual machines or self-managed Kubernetes to EKS.
  • Hardening an existing EKS cluster for availability, security, observability, and release safety.
  • Building a reusable delivery checklist for platform or DevOps teams.

Do not use this as:

  • A beginner Kubernetes tutorial.
  • A short-lived proof-of-concept guide.
  • A replacement for multi-Region disaster recovery architecture.
  • A complete regulated-industry compliance blueprint.

Multi-AZ inside one Region is not the same as multi-Region resilience. Treat those as different architecture problems.

docs.title: Assumptions
docs.text:
This standard assumes:

  • One AWS Region with at least three Availability Zones.
  • Mostly stateless services, with state placed in managed data services or carefully designed persistent storage.
  • GitOps or CI/CD based delivery.
  • Public or internal ingress traffic.
  • Least-privilege access, auditability, and rollback requirements.

For a small low-risk environment, reduce the design: two capacity classes, one ingress path, basic metrics, basic logs, and explicit backup. Do not add every production component just to make the platform look mature.

docs.title: Target Architecture
docs.text:
Users
  |
Route 53
  |
AWS Load Balancer Controller
  |
Ingress / Service
  |
Application Pods
  |
Managed data services / persistent storage

Control and operations plane:

EKS managed control plane
  - multi-AZ managed by AWS

Worker capacity
  - managed node groups or Karpenter
  - spread across at least 3 AZs
  - separate system and application capacity

Identity
  - IAM roles for cluster operators
  - EKS Pod Identity or IRSA for workload permissions

Observability
  - metrics
  - logs
  - events
  - alerts
  - audit trail

The key principle: AWS manages the EKS control plane, but application availability still depends on your VPC, subnets, node groups, scheduling rules, Pod replicas, disruption budgets, storage, ingress, and release process.

docs.title: 1. Multi-AZ Is The Baseline
docs.text:
A production EKS cluster should normally use at least three AZs. Two AZs can be acceptable for lower-risk systems, but capacity recovery and maintenance flexibility are weaker. A one-AZ EKS cluster should not be described as production high availability.

Check:

  • The VPC spans at least three AZs.
  • Each AZ has private subnets for worker nodes.
  • Node groups or Karpenter provision capacity across AZs.
  • Application replicas are spread with topology spread constraints or anti-affinity.
  • Storage classes are understood in terms of AZ binding.

Common failure mode: the cluster is configured with three AZ subnets, but actual worker capacity only exists in one AZ. The architecture diagram says multi-AZ; the scheduler experiences a single-AZ platform.

docs.title: 2. Worker Capacity Should Be Layered
docs.text:
Do not run every Pod on the same worker pool. A minimum production model has:

  • System capacity: CoreDNS, ingress controllers, log agents, monitoring agents.
  • Application capacity: user-facing and internal services.
  • Batch or Spot capacity: retryable jobs and interruptible workloads.

The purpose is not complexity. The purpose is to prevent business traffic or batch spikes from evicting the components that keep the cluster observable and reachable.

Recommended principles:

  • Keep system components on stable capacity.
  • Select instance families based on workload resource profiles.
  • Use Spot only for workloads that tolerate interruption and retry.
  • Use PodDisruptionBudgets for critical services.

docs.title: 3. Pods Should Not Inherit Broad Node Permissions
docs.text:
Production workloads should not rely on the node IAM role for AWS API access. Use EKS Pod Identity or IRSA so each workload gets the minimum IAM role it needs.

Acceptance criteria:

  • Each workload receives only the AWS permissions it needs.
  • Secrets, object storage, queues, and container registry permissions are separated.
  • Permission changes are auditable.
  • Removing an application also removes or disables its cloud permissions.

Common failure mode: S3, ECR, CloudWatch, and Secrets Manager permissions are all attached to the node role. It is convenient at first, but any compromised Pod can inherit excessive lateral movement capability.

docs.title: 4. Ingress Is More Than An Ingress Object
docs.text:
A production ingress path needs decisions for:

  • DNS ownership.
  • TLS issuance and renewal.
  • Public or internal load balancers.
  • WAF requirements.
  • Stable health check paths.
  • Traffic shifting for rollback or canary releases.

If a design only contains an Ingress YAML file, the ingress layer is not finished.

docs.title: 5. Default To Managed Services For State
docs.text:
EKS can run stateful systems, but production teams should not default to placing every database, queue, search engine, or cache inside the cluster. Prefer managed services such as RDS, ElastiCache, MSK, OpenSearch, and S3 where they fit.

Before running stateful services in the cluster, answer:

  • Where are backups stored?
  • How does recovery work across AZ failure?
  • How are failed upgrades rolled back?
  • What happens when a node is lost?
  • Does the team really have operational ownership of this component?

docs.title: Phase 1: Network And Account Boundary
docs.text:
Deliver:

  • VPC, subnets, route tables, NAT Gateway or an approved alternative.
  • Cluster IAM role.
  • Node IAM role.
  • Operator and CI/CD access boundaries.
  • Tagging standards.

Validate:

aws sts get-caller-identity
aws ec2 describe-subnets --filters "Name=vpc-id,Values=<vpc-id>"
aws eks describe-cluster --name <cluster-name>

Risks: NAT Gateway cost is often underestimated; private cluster access is often forgotten; overly broad administrator access tends to become the default.

docs.title: Phase 2: Create The EKS Control Plane
docs.text:
Deliver:

  • EKS cluster.
  • Kubernetes version selection.
  • API server endpoint access policy.
  • Control plane logging.
  • Encryption configuration.

Validate:

aws eks update-kubeconfig --name <cluster-name> --region <region>
kubectl cluster-info
kubectl get nodes
kubectl get ns

Note: kubectl get nodes can be empty immediately after the control plane is created. A healthy control plane does not mean worker capacity has joined.

docs.title: Phase 3: Worker Capacity And System Components
docs.text:
Deliver:

  • Managed Node Groups or Karpenter.
  • Verified versions for CoreDNS, kube-proxy, and VPC CNI.
  • Scheduling separation for system and application components.
  • Cluster Autoscaler or Karpenter scale-out behavior.

Validate:

kubectl get nodes -o wide
kubectl -n kube-system get pods -o wide
kubectl describe node <node-name>
kubectl top nodes

Failure mode: nodes are Ready, but business Pods stay Pending because subnet IP capacity or CNI configuration is exhausted. The broken object is not the Deployment; it is the network capacity model.

docs.title: Phase 4: Identity, Permissions, And Secrets
docs.text:
Deliver:

  • EKS Pod Identity or IRSA.
  • Least-privilege IAM policies.
  • Secret management rules.
  • CI/CD deployment permissions.

Validate:

kubectl -n <namespace> describe serviceaccount <service-account>
kubectl -n <namespace> exec deploy/<deployment-name> -- aws sts get-caller-identity

The identity visible from the business Pod should be the workload role, not the node role.

docs.title: Phase 5: Ingress, Certificates, And Release Path
docs.text:
Deliver:

  • AWS Load Balancer Controller.
  • IngressClass.
  • DNS records.
  • TLS certificates.
  • Health checks.
  • Canary or blue-green release strategy.

Validate:

kubectl -n kube-system get deploy aws-load-balancer-controller
kubectl -n <namespace> get ingress
kubectl -n <namespace> describe ingress <ingress-name>
curl -I https://<service-domain>

Failure mode: the Ingress object is created, but no load balancer appears. The usual causes are controller permissions, subnet tags, or incorrect annotations.

docs.title: Phase 6: Observability And Alerting
docs.text:
Deliver:

  • Node metrics.
  • Pod metrics.
  • Application logs.
  • Kubernetes Events.
  • API server or audit logs.
  • Critical alerts.

Minimum alert set:

  • Node NotReady.
  • Pod CrashLoopBackOff.
  • Deployment replica shortage.
  • PVC high usage.
  • Ingress 5xx increase.
  • HPA unable to scale for a sustained period.
  • CoreDNS error rate increase.

Validate:

kubectl get events -A --sort-by=.lastTimestamp
kubectl top pods -A
kubectl -n <monitoring-namespace> get pods

docs.title: Rollback Strategy
docs.text:
EKS rollback cannot rely only on kubectl rollout undo. Roll back by layer.

Layer Rollback Method Application release Revert image tag, Helm release, or GitOps commit Ingress Restore old rule, old certificate, or old load balancer Worker nodes Revert launch template or create a previous-version node group Permissions Revert IAM policy or ServiceAccount binding Cluster add-ons Revert to a compatible add-on version or temporarily scale old capacity Kubernetes version Usually cannot be downgraded in place; recover through a new cluster migration plan

The Kubernetes version upgrade path is often underestimated. Before upgrading production, confirm compatibility for add-ons, node AMIs, controllers, and critical CRDs.

docs.title: Production Acceptance Checklist
docs.text:
Before production traffic:

  • The cluster spans three AZs.
  • Private subnet IP capacity is sufficient.
  • Worker capacity spans AZs and can recover automatically.
  • System and application components have capacity separation.
  • CoreDNS has enough replicas and is spread across nodes.
  • Critical applications have multiple replicas, PDBs, requests, and limits.
  • Ingress has DNS, TLS, health checks, and rollback.
  • Workloads use least-privilege identity.
  • Secrets are not stored in images, ConfigMaps, or Git.
  • Logs, metrics, events, and alerts are available.
  • CI/CD permissions are auditable.
  • Backup and restore have been tested.
  • Upgrade and failure drills are recorded.

docs.title: Mistake 1: Treating The Managed Control Plane As Application HA
docs.text: AWS reduces control plane operational burden, but application Pods, node capacity, ingress, storage, delivery, and monitoring remain your responsibility. Application availability is designed and verified, not purchased by cluster creation.

docs.title: Mistake 2: Counting Nodes Without Checking Placement
docs.text: Six nodes in one AZ still create an AZ-level failure risk. Replica count and node count only matter when combined with topology distribution.

docs.title: Mistake 3: Migrating Stateful Services First
docs.text: Start migrations with stateless services. Use them to prove image standards, release flow, ingress, logging, metrics, and rollback. Stateful services come later because their hardest problems are data consistency, recovery time, and recovery point.

docs.title: Mistake 4: Leaving Permissions To The End
docs.text: If permissions are handled last, temporary exceptions become permanent patterns. Pod identity and deployment access boundaries should exist from the start.

docs.title: Q&A Assistant Blocks
docs.text:
Question: Is a Managed Node Group enough for EKS high availability?

No. Managed Node Groups help with worker lifecycle management, but high availability also depends on multi-AZ subnets, scheduling spread, Pod replicas, PDBs, ingress, storage, permissions, monitoring, and rollback.

Question: How many AZs should a production EKS cluster use?

Usually at least three AZs. Two AZs can work for lower-risk systems, but recovery and maintenance flexibility are weaker. A one-AZ cluster should not be called production high availability.

Question: How should Pods access AWS services?

Use EKS Pod Identity or IRSA and bind each workload to a least-privilege IAM role. Do not let application Pods inherit broad permissions from the node role.

Question: Should stateful services run inside EKS?

Prefer managed AWS services by default. Run stateful systems inside EKS only when the team owns backup, recovery, upgrade, monitoring, and incident handling for that component.
